data.title: Five Below's On-Trend Merchandise May Now Be Priced In
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Five Below (FIVE), I explained why its CEO Winnie Park was doing a great job with her merchandising strategy and why I expected the company to outperform analysts’ estimates. I think the CEO deserves credit for this discount store’s stronger-than-expected performance this quarter, because it’s not just competing on price. It’s on trend. And that’s important when you’re competing for younger shoppers who only want to spend money at the stores they love the most.

In the third quarter, Five Below reported that revenue rose 23.1% to $1.04 billion. The company reported 14.3% comps after guiding for comps in the 5%-7% range last quarter. As you might expect, it raised its guidance and now expects 6% to 8% comps in the fourth quarter and 9.4% to 10.1% comps for the full year. EPS also rose from $0.03 in 3Q 2024 to $0.66 in 3Q 2025. So, Five Below is doing great right now, and now investors can decide whether to take profits here or hold onto the stock. Obviously, expectations for Five Below are higher now. But so are its in-store prices.

Five Below’s Still Introducing Higher Price Points

On the call, Park said, “Today, value is not just about $5 and below, but ensuring we pack value into $7, $10 and $15-plus items.” This pricing strategy is obviously a risk for a store that primarily appeals to low-income parents who are trying to save money on toys. But it appears that the store’s customers understand why Five Below needs to raise its prices, and the CEO says that they’ve responded well. Five Below is trying to keep its prices on holiday merchandise affordable, but it has incurred additional expenses because of other factors, such as tariffs and labor costs.

Like I explained last time, Five Below is also using a “treasure hunt” strategy with its higher-priced merchandise. The company’s higher-priced Five Beyond products are now distributed throughout Five Below stores, so there’s no longer a separate Five Beyond department. Apparently, this strategy worked. It sounds like the new product placement strategy makes it easier for the company to focus on broader themes, such as Christmas, rather than just focusing on discounts.

Five Below also reported that it had more customers this quarter, and its customers spent more money. On the call, Park also said, “We were pleased to see this growth was led equally by increases in both comparable transactions and comparable tickets.” Right now, some stores are only reporting comps growth because they raised prices, and their traffic is down. But that’s not the case for this store.

Five Below Is Also Stockpiling Inventory

If you look at Five Below’s balance sheet, the company’s inventory rose 36% to $1.112 billion this quarter. On the call, the CEO also said that the average Five Below store is carrying 25% more inventory right now. The official explanation for this is that Five Below is stockpiling more inventory because of tariffs. But I also think that this rise in inventory could indicate additional downside or additional upside.

The bullish argument is that Five Below plans to sell its merchandise for higher prices in the future. The company will have to pay higher prices to its suppliers for products that it will sell for $10 or $15 than it would pay for products it will sell for $5. Buying these products would also increase the reported value of its inventory. So, the rise in inventory could just indicate that Five Below will maintain a pricing strategy that has already increased its comps in the first 3 quarters of 2025.

The bearish argument is that Five Below may have bought higher-priced products that its customers can’t afford. Because inventory is an asset, if the company did this, its bottom-line results would still look okay in the short term. But Five Below might have to sell its excess inventory at a discount in upcoming quarters, or even write it off entirely, and that would reduce its margins. It doesn’t look like this is happening now, but the company did write off some unsold inventory last year. So, inventory write-offs in 4Q 2025 are a risk factor for Five Below.

Five Below Has an Easy Comparison Next Quarter

Five Below’s guidance might be too low again next quarter because it has an easy comparison coming up. Winnie Park joined the company last fall, so she wouldn’t have had time to make major changes to the company’s merchandising strategy or marketing plan. So, the company was likely still following its old strategies in 4Q 2024, and its performance that quarter wasn’t great. Five Below posted -3.0% comps in 4Q 2024. But now, after reporting 14.3% comps in 3Q 2025, it’s guiding for 6%-8% comps in 4Q 2025. With an easy comparison, higher prices, and better strategies, it’s likely to surpass expectations again. Five Below’s marketing has also improved recently.

Five Below Is Now Investing in Social Media Marketing

Deep discounters, such as dollar stores, often spend very little on marketing to keep their prices low. A store that’s using the treasure-hunt strategy doesn’t really need to promote specific products, and its inventory will change frequently anyway. But now Five Below is planning to spend money on social media marketing, which makes it more like a regular toy store. Social media marketing will also make it possible for this store to collect more data on its customers than other dollar stores have.

Right now, Five Below’s mystery dumpling toys are getting attention on social media platforms such as TikTok and YouTube. The mystery dumplings are collectibles, and some of them are harder to find than others, so kids have incentives to buy more mystery boxes. And content creators are creating unboxing videos for the mystery dumplings as well. So, it appears that Five Below’s social media strategy is working well right now.

The Main Risk Is That Five Below Will Raise Its Prices Too Much

Many consumers still consider Five Below to be a dollar store like Dollar Tree (DLTR) that sells inexpensive imported products and cheap snacks. While other dollar stores are also experimenting with higher price points right now, Five Below may struggle if it begins stocking too many products at higher price points. A consumer who’s willing to spend $15 on a single item might decide to shop at an off-price store like The TJX Companies' (TJX) T. J. Maxx or HomeGoods instead.

Five Below’s Comparative Valuation Is Still Reasonable

I’ll compare Five Below to TJX and Ross Stores (ROST) again, like I did last time. After the market close on December 23, Five Below had a forward EV/EBITDA of 19.20 and a forward EV/sales of 2.54. On the same date, TJX had a forward EV/EBITDA of 22.55 and a forward EV/sales of 3.04. And Ross had a forward EV/EBITDA of 18.89 and a forward EV/sales of 2.65. So, even after the rally, Five Below is reasonably valued in comparison to peers, although the other discount stores are also trading at high multiples right now. And other dollar stores’ margins are also rising right now.

My Updated Valuation for Five Below

Five Below raised its guidance this quarter, and its 2025 guidance may still be low. So, I’ll use the top end of the company’s revised 2025 guidance this time. Its guidance calls for $4.65 billion in revenue and adjusted net income of $327 million, so its profit margin would be 7.0%. If I divide its TTM net income of $307.9 million by its TTM revenue of $4.427 billion, it has a 7.0% profit margin right now. So, I’ll divide its TTM EBITDA of $581.9 million by its TTM revenue to get a full-year EBITDA margin of 13.1%.

Last time, I projected that Five Below’s revenue would get a 10% uplift from new stores in 2026 and a 5% uplift from comps. But because of recent price increases, its comps may rise faster than I expected. So, I’ll project that it will get a 7% uplift from comps in 2026 and its full-year revenue will rise 17%. My updated estimate for 2026 sales would then be $5.120 billion. With fixed cost leverage from additional sales growth, I’ll project that its EBITDA margin will rise to 13.4% as well. So, I’d estimate that Five Below’s EBITDA will be $686 million in 2026.

At a 2026 EV/EBITDA of 18, Five Below’s EV would be $12.348 billion. After subtracting $2.01 billion in debt and adding back $525 million in cash, it would have a market cap of $10.863 billion. Five Below’s share count increased from 55.2 million to 55.3 million in 2025, so for 2026 I’ll project that its share count will rise to 55.4 million diluted shares. Dividing a market cap of $10.863 billion by 55.4 million shares gives me a one-year target price of $196 per share. That’s 4% higher than the current price of $188 per share. And the SA Quant score says Five Below is a hold right now.

Conclusion

My conclusion is that if you read my previous article and bought stock in Five Below, you should take profit now. This is still a well-managed store. Winnie Park has improved the company’s merchandising strategy by adding new products that appeal to kids. It’s clear that Asian-inspired toys like the mystery dumplings are appealing to social media users as well. And Five Below’s customers also appear to have taken the store’s price increases well. But these factors are now priced in. So, I downgrade Five Below to a hold.

data.title: Applied Optoelectronics: The Breakout Nobody Is Watching
data.description:
Investment Thesis

For so long, Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI) was a ticker based on a transition narrative that had nothing to prove itself but was just somewhere in between traditional optics and what it claimed was hyperscalability. Since my last coverage, AAOI has skyrocketed because the company showed that its technology can address actual AI datacenter bottlenecks.

Its latest product, 400mW narrow-linewidth pump laser, is capable of delivering 800G/1.6T silicon photonics and co-packaged optics because of its high and stable optical power, overcoming power budget constraints which existing solutions cannot address and proving AAOI's advantage in this area of hyperscalers.

However, the most important development was the first volume order received for 800G hyperscalers. I believe this milestone resolves AAOI’s core uncertainty and shifts the debate from if the company belongs in AI infrastructure to how efficiently it can scale revenue, expand margins, and convert capacity into durable cash flows.

The Qualification Risk Is Gone, Execution Risk Is Not

The most significant aspect of the order from AAOI’s perspective is not the volume of the initial order of 800G but what it represents. Being qualified in the area of hyperscalers in the optical networking space is an intensive multiple year effort with a high degree of friction that is intended to eliminate the weakest suppliers in the ecosystem. Being past this hurdle qualifies AAOI not only on the basis of performance but also reliability and cost competitiveness. A significant number of optical companies qualify in the latter stages of the process. Fewer of them proceed to volume shipments.

Until December, AAOI's future was reliant on a binary outcome. A failure to build volume from qualification would have put it at risk from slower growth legacy markets. However, now that volume has begun to ship, this critical risk has been mitigated. The new risk profile is certainly more manageable, though it is no less tricky. How does AAOI plan to accomplish manufacturing and yield without sacrificing pricing and driving margins higher?

I also see a strong duration component being realized. Once integrated within hyperscale network architectures, vendors often stick around across multiple speed generations. Having a 800G presence now significantly ups the odds of being present in 1.6T deployments in the next generation, which is hard to value within short-term models.

A quick reminder, the 800G and 1.6T optics are critical because AI workloads require massive, low-latency data movement between GPUs, and slower interconnects quickly become bottlenecks. As clusters scale, bandwidth per rack must double without exploding power and cost. Applied Optoelectronics is well positioned because of its vertically integrated lasers and early hyperscaler qualification, allowing faster scaling and cost control. While competitors are close, AAOI is already shipping, which puts it slightly ahead on execution rather than technology alone.

What to Watch Next: The Proof Points That Matter After the 800G Volume Order

After the first 800G volume order, I believe the hardest part of the story is no longer belief but follow-through. Applied Optoelectronics has crossed the qualification barrier and now it must prove that this was not a one-off event but the start of a repeatable revenue engine. What gives me comfort is that management has been unusually specific about what should happen next. The roadmap is operational, not aspirational.

The initial confirmation point is Q4 revenue coming in. Q3 revenue was impacted by timing issues, not demand. Approximately $6.6 million of shipped 400G product was not recognized as revenue due to quarter-end logistics and rolled into Q4. The company also said Q4 would come in at $125–140 million in revenue, saying that the data center could grow an adequate amount of revenue organically by $25 to 40 million sequentially. In my view, hitting even the midpoint would validate that Q3 weakness was accounting noise, not a demand issue. I am far less concerned with the exact headline number than with whether data center revenue inflects decisively, as guided.

The second major component of confirmation is product mix progress. In Q3, more than 80% of data center sales were from 100G products, with limited contribution from 200G and 400G transceivers and no material contribution from 800G. That is a backward-looking indicator. Importantly, management has indicated 800G is imminent and should start seeing substantial 800G sales in Q4. That is important because 800G sales have substantially higher ASPs. A tiny number of 800G sales can have a disproportionately large effect on revenue. I will track closely for signs of 400G and 800G sales beginning to displace 100G sales in the mix.

The third confirmation point is the momentum in shipment volumes. The management team has articulated a sharp ramp, from 10-20 thousand units a month currently to 100 thousand by the end of the year and 200 thousand in mid-2026. This represents roughly a 15–16x increase in capacity over a relatively short timeframe for a company of its size. While I don't expect flawless execution, verification of the trajectory toward these goals would indicate that the growth engine is firing as intended despite revenue phasing.

The fourth confirmation point would be margin stability rather than expansion at this stage. The guidance on gross margin improvement in Q4 came in at 29-31%, which was below what the market was eagerly anticipating. In my view, the response of the stocks was wrong here. Typically, initial growth phase margins tend to contract as a consequence of learning curves and mix considerations. The key for now is margin stability as revenue momentum gathers pace.

Finally, the fact that customer breadth is the most underappreciated indicator is a positive note. The management has indicated that there are multiple additional customers that are qualifying at the 800G level at a late stage. Having a second hyperscale company indicate that they are approaching volume shipment levels would significantly mitigate concerns about concentration risk and would further cement the view that the current ramp is structural rather than a company-specific trend.

Valuation Through the Right Lens

At a stock price of $40, AAOI is no longer considered a cheap stock. The P/E ratios based on past earnings are also not meaningful because the firm is continuing to lose money. The market is valuing AAOI on forward numbers. Looking at FY2026 numbers, the stock is quoting at about 48x forward earnings. For a mature hardware company, it would be hard to explain that kind of pricing. When you are emerging from a loss phase into a growth phase, it’s not that pricey.

Revenue multiples are more informative. AAOI currently has a forward multiple of about 6x on FY2025 estimates, reducing to 3.6x on FY2026 figures if the uplift is delivered. The latter rate suggests that the market is not baking in strong out-year forecasts so much, but is rather pre-paying for their delivery and waiting for the goods.

Compared to optical stocks with more conservative growth prospects in terms of trader activity in the 3-5x sales range, AAOI appears rich but not necessarily unconcerned with fundamentals. This stock valuation can more accurately be described as a bet upon execution over the 12-18 month forward period.

Where the Risks Actually Sit

Despite the improved setup, Applied Optoelectronics remains a high-risk, high-beta investment. The most important risk is execution. Scaling production from early volumes to mass deployment can expose yield issues, supply-chain bottlenecks, or labor constraints that delay revenue and pressure margins.

Customer concentration is another concern. Near-term 800G demand is likely driven by a small number of hyperscalers. Any pause in capital spending or procurement delays could impact results quickly. Pricing pressure also remains structural. Hyperscalers exert strong negotiating power, and aggressive competition could limit AAOI’s ability to reach its targeted mid-30% gross margins.

Takeaway

I think AAOI is beyond the speculation stage and into implementation. The 800G volume order eliminates any spark of key uncertainty; now it is just sound execution. If the management can follow through on Q4 inflection points, volume growth, and mix changes, volatility is just background noise.

data.title: Worthington Enterprises: Upgrade To Buy On Improved Fundamentals
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Worthington Enterprises (WOR) was a hold rating because of the hurdles I mentioned, especially the consumer product segment, which remains a drag. Q2 2026 showed WOR can grow earnings even with macro pressure. Building Products is growing organically, Consumer Products is stabilizing with distribution wins, and the portfolio mix is improving. So long as WOR shows clearer evidence of recovery and data center-related revenue continues to grow, valuation should go up.

Q2 2026 Earnings Review

Previously, I said investors should be patient until there was clearer evidence the business could perform despite the macro pressure. WOR delivered on this. Q2 2026 net sales grew 19% y/y to $327.5 million, with Building Products net sales up >30% y/y to $207.5 million, and importantly, Consumer Products was up 2.7% y/y to ~$120 million. Volumes are still under pressure, but the pace of decline appears to be stabilizing. Margins may seem weak on the surface. Consolidated gross margin fell 120 bps to 25.8%, but context matters. The weakness was largely due to the Elgen integration, which WOR needed to invest in safety upgrades, new equipment, and ~40 incremental hires to drive up productivity. These are upfront costs that weigh on the P&L since the expected revenue has not flowed through. Adjusting for these integration noises, the earnings profile looks alright, with adjusted EPS at $0.65.

Building Products

My view previously was that WOR’s Building Products segment was taking share, but I get that some bearish investors would think that is due to the acquisition and not organic share gains. WOR proved me right in this Q2 quarter. I mean, for sure, the Elgen acquisition did drive up the headline numbers, but even if we strip that away, organic sales in Building Products are growing at ~16% y/y. This is a very clean datapoint that tells us WOR is gaining share since the broader construction demand remains muted. This organic performance also proved what I said about the tailwinds is right. Specifically, the regulatory shift toward A2L refrigerants is catalyzing growth, and WOR early supply preparation has paid off.

The LSI Group acquisition makes the story even better. As a recap, WOR acquired LSI for ~$205 million, and there are two levels of impact here. One is that LSI immediately lifts WOR’s margin profile since LSI operates at ~40% adjusted EBITDA margin. The second, and more important, angle is a strategic one. For context, LSI specializes in metal roof clips, a product used mainly for fixing or replacing old roofs rather than building new ones. This nuance matters because it opens the door for WOR to further capture demand from renovations/repairs vs. new homes/buildings built. For new homes built, a developer can easily cancel a new building project if financing costs get too high (which is the situation we are in today, where rates are high and the macro outlook is uncertain). However, a building owner cannot ignore a leaking roof. Hence, by pivoting toward this maintenance work (that is essential), WOR effectively insulates itself from the current interest rate cycle, in my view.

WAVE Resilience, Consumer Wins, and the Data Center Pivot

The other part of the aspects of the bull case includes the WAVE joint venture, the potential upside in the Consumer Products segment, and data centers. Of the three, I saw WAVE as a cash generator for WOR, and this is still true. In Q2 2026, despite the fact that commercial construction remains muted, WAVE still delivered equity earnings of $26.3 million (vs. $24.6 million last year). This itself should ease the bears' concern that WAVE could become a cash drag in the current macro environment.

For the Consumer Products segment, this was one of the hurdles I noted WOR needed to clear before I would turn bullish. As a recap, this segment was weighed down by poor demand in core categories (camping gas and tools), mainly due to the poor consumer spending environment. But I believe the tide may be shifting. Firstly, consumer spending appears to be more robust than expected. Secondly, WOR has announced a nationwide distribution win with Costco (COST), which will begin carrying Balloon Time products this spring. I take this as a “stamp of approval” by one of the biggest retail names in the industry. My view is that COST doesn’t add shelf space for no reason. They must have seen a good level of demand (for those products); if not, it doesn’t make sense. The fact that management also noted total store count placement is up 63% y/y tells me that this is not just an isolated event with COST. If the current consumer spending momentum continues, the rebound in this segment could be a lot larger because of the expanded footprint.

The last point here is data centers, and this is where I think there is huge value. Because this is still a small revenue contributor, the market seems to be ignoring it. I believe the impact could be a lot more meaningful in the coming years. The reality today is that data centers are facing massive supply chain issues, and I don’t think that is going to ease given the structural need for more AI capabilities. Management was also explicit in Q2 that demand is rising across liquid cooling water tanks, WAVE structural grids, and Elgen strut systems. These are not “good-to-have” solutions but are necessary to ensure a data center can work.

And I think the importance of these products goes up over time. For AI data centers, especially, high-density GPU racks generate a significant amount of heat, which forces operators to invest heavily in liquid cooling. This is where WOR’s water tanks come into play. As for those racks (which include cabling and power systems), they require stronger structural support and ceiling grids. This is where the WAVE and Elgen contribute. As you can see, these are not discretionary expenses. They are a necessity.

The impact of valuation should become more distinct over time as this segment grows to a bigger mix. As I see it today, investors are attaching much higher multiples for companies that have data center exposure. For example, Eaton Corp. (ETN) trades at 23x NTM PE, Schneider Electric trades at 24x, and Vertiv Holdings (VRT) trades at 32x. But WOR only trades at 14x NTM PE. As this mix scales, I expect the narrative to change. Data center-related revenue should dominate much of WOR’s headline, and that should start to influence how it is being valued.

Valuation

I see a path for WOR’s shares to be worth $63. The Building Product segment is doing well, the Consumer segment (a key hurdle I identified) should start to see a rebound, and the exposure to Data Center adds a strong growth driver to the equity story. Year-to-date, WOR has grown revenue by high teens (mainly because of the acquisitions made). Assuming WOR grows by mid-teens in FY26, followed by mid-single-digit growth, which is tough to believe given the organic growth in the core Building Product segment and other aspects of things I mentioned, and that margin stays flat. WOR should do $195 million in adj. earnings in FY28. Say that valuation goes up to 16x NTM PE (still a sharp discount vs peers with exposure to data centers), I get to a share price target of $63.

Risk

The integration risk with the Elgen and LSI group may be harder than expected, causing WOR to allocate more resources to it (thereby delaying the growth recovery timeline). Consumer spending confidence may also stay weak, unlike what I believed, and this may result in the consumer product segment seeing delayed recovery as well.

Conclusion

I upgrade my rating to a buy. Building Products is gaining share with structural tailwinds, and Consumer Products is positioned for a rebound on a much larger footprint. The WAVE JV still generates a good amount of cash, and the data center exposure adds a strong growth driver to the thesis. At ~14x NTM PE, I see a path for multiple expansion as well.

data.title: FBTC: 2 Reasons Why I Am Bullish, And 1 No For 2026
data.description:
Today, I go back to looking at Bitcoin (BTC-USD) with bullish tones, always on Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund ETF (FBTC) (the instrument I prefer for short-term operations) in view of the fact that there could be a contrarian operation on the table not to be taken lightly. I glimpse between the lines 2 reasons to be optimistic about Bitcoin looking ahead to 2026, and 1 reason to be fearful. But before getting into the heart of the situation, I’ll leave you with a …

Short summary of previous analyses

For new readers, I’m happy to leave you a list of the key information to know about FBTC:

  • Let’s start from the fact that FBTC’s objective is to replicate the performance of spot Bitcoin; in doing so, it uses the Fidelity Bitcoin Reference Rate as its benchmark.

  • The ETF holds physical Bitcoin and uses Fidelity Digital Asset Services (FDAS) as Custodian; custody is split between cold + hot storage.
  • It has a Net expense ratio of 0.25%, which is paid in Bitcoin (an element that in the long term fuels the classic fee drag), against a truly negligible Bid/ask spread (30d median) of around ~0.05%. The TER is convenient and ranks in the 97th-98th percentile according to Fidelity, therefore among the cheapest.

  • Not by chance, it has Net assets of around ~$18.22 billion, which makes it widely accepted globally as a traditional-market alternative to Bitcoin: as a consequence, and not to be taken for granted, it is also strong in volumetric terms.

  • For this reason, in my latest coverage, in the comparison with peers such as IBIT, BTC and HODL, I considered FBTC an interesting solution for faster Bitcoin trading operations.

My opinion

Bitcoin is a dynamic asset, both in terms of price action and in terms of identity. This means that in certain contexts it is an equity-like solution, and tends to move in line with the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq; at times it is a safe haven asset, and is associated by supporters with digital gold; at other times instead it is detached from any narrative, and moves following its own dynamics, the on-chain ones. So … looking for answers within these 3 identified boxes, I personally identified 2 reasons to be bullish on Bitcoin, and 1 reason not to be (which we will identify as a risk):

Macro point of view

Although, in my view, it is the least sensitive dimension for Bitcoin, the macro context is a positive factor today in my opinion, and the reason can be summarized in a series of elements:

  • Deterioration of trust in fiat currencies.
  • Increase in U.S. fiscal deficits.
  • The Fed has turned dovish with a restarted rate-cut cycle.

If real rates remain in decline, there will be a progressive reduction in the attractiveness of bonds and cash.

Here comes into play the dimension of Bitcoin described as a safe haven asset, a parallel solution to Gold:

  • hedge against inflation.
  • hedge against central bank policy errors.

And personally, today I would also add to this dimension the decisive role of the Trump administration; an administration that nonetheless remains

  • pro-crypto and pro-deregulation.
  • It introduced the CLARITY Act and the GENIUS Act and is moving toward the formation of a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve (without direct purchases).

On-chain point of view

The second dimension lies within models that attempt to describe Bitcoin’s price action based on technical evidence or data that can only be read on-chain. In these models, Bitcoin’s price is located in an intermediate-low area relative to the secular trend. Specifically:

  • Stock to flow is not working as it should; since 2022, Bitcoin’s price (even if bullish) has not been in line with the average defined by the model, which is based on the concept of scarcity. From one point of view it can be associated with a failure of the model, while from another it can also be seen as a dimension of undervaluation.

  • Hashrate does not give operational signals, but in my opinion it gives an idea of the sentiment of market operators. In recent years hashrate has grown significantly, and for this reason it is synonymous with greater interest in market participation by new players. And who would invest in a market destined to die? In my opinion, very few.

  • We are also in the lower range on logarithmic models; my absolute favorite is the rainbow chart, which still places the price among the lower horizons of the chart.

  • We are in minimum zones also from a technical point of view, if we look at the RSI, below the 50-point level.

Risk

It only remains to question the equity-like nature of Bitcoin. This dimension, for me, is the most important one. With the process of institutionalization of the network (which occurred through the deregulation itself of the Trump administration, and began with the launch of ETFs), Bitcoin has increasingly taken on a strong anchoring to traditional indices. In particular, the index of tech companies, the Nasdaq index.

And this was true until October 2025. Then Bitcoin took on a separate dimension. And this is an element that personally worries me because, while on one hand the price targets and expectations of investment banks on the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq for 2026 are bullish, on Bitcoin this has become a doubt given the decline in the correlation index. The question that naturally arises for me is: could the classic negative halving cycle be underway?

Conclusion

To conclude, despite or thanks to the declines from the highs recorded by FBTC, I begin to glimpse 2 positive elements: on the macro and on-chain fronts. Elements that could stimulate demand, making FBTC a profitable instrument in the short to medium term. At the same time, the disconnection from traditional markets is an element that I personally do not appreciate, and it makes investment choices disorienting.

data.title: What The 'Experts' Got Wrong In 2025
data.description:
2025 is coming to a rapid close. It has been another year of outsized gains from the major indexes. However, most of the gains have once again been powered by huge AI-related plays like Alphabet (GOOG), which is up 65% for the year, and Nvidia Corporation (NVDA), which has tacked on another 40% to its huge gains in recent years.

Appreciation from the S&P 493 has been much harder to come by. The Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP) has climbed just over 11%, including dividends, to date in 2025. So, before I pen an upcoming article on what is likely ahead for the markets in 2026, let's look back on three key items the "experts" got wrong in 2025.

Overall S&P 500 Returns:

Once again, the S&P 500 (SP500) exceeded almost all forecasted returns and is up nearly 19% year to date, including its record low dividend yield. This follows annual returns of just over 25% in 2023 and 2024 for the S&P 500.

A great year for sure, but is this surge in equity indexes to continue in 2026? Only the "Shadow knows," as my late father liked to quip.

Another big rally in the markets in 2026 is hard to see occurring given the current valuations of the overall market. If the rally is to continue, the advance needs to broaden in my opinion. To do that, the S&P 493 has to produce far above the 3% annual profit growth it has averaged annually from 2023-2025.

Tariff Impacts:

The overwhelming economic consensus when reciprocal tariffs were announced in early April, is they would lead to a surge in inflation and trigger a significant slowdown in the U.S., perhaps even pushing the economy into a recession. The news spurred a brief but sharp selloff in the stock market to start the second quarter.

While the CPI is still over the Federal Reserve's two percent target, it has not moved higher as the experts expected. In addition, GDP growth in Q2 was 3.9% and last week it was announced that Q3 GDP initial growth came in at a robust 4.3%. This is the strongest quarterly reading in two years. Growth was powered by robust consumer spending, a return to growth in government spending after two quarters of decline (all powered by an increase in defense spending, non-defense spending continued to fall), rising exports (adding 1.6% to Q3 growth) and falling imports (down 4.7% year-over-year).

To be fair, actual tariffs implemented on most countries have been lower than feared when reciprocal tariffs were initially announced. And the CPI is still running at near three percent. Also, of note. A surge of tariff revenues, estimated at $300 billion in FY2026, is one of the few positives on the fiscal budgeting front as well.

Precious Metals Going Parabolic:

One thing no "expert" I know predicted at the start of 2025 was the parabolic rise in precious metals. Silver is up approximately 150% here in 2025. Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is up better than 60% year to date and also at all-time highs.

The fact these price surges are happening with robust economic growth, inflation at under three percent, and the equity markets at/near all-time highs; is disconcerting. The rallies in precious metals signal increasing worries about the massive sovereign debt throughout the G-20 as well as the longer-term value of the dollar. These huge rises in precious metal prices could also reflect heightened concerns about the health of the overall credit markets.

Conclusion:

What will the "experts" get wrong in 2026? That is something we will find out together in the coming 12 months. My take is stock market returns are going to be very hard to come by in 2026. First, the forward P/E ratio on the S&P 500 is currently 23.18x. Of note, every time the forward P/E ratio for this index has exceeded 23x in U.S. history, the subsequent annual equity return over the next decade has been between +2% to -2%.

Second, 2026 is a midterm year. Over the past century, the S&P 500 has seen an average drawdown of 18.2% in the 12 months before midterm elections. Over the past 60 years, the smallest drawdown has been 7.4%. The largest was 41.8%. Of note, the last annual decline for the S&P 500 was in the mid-term year of 2022. The index fell just over 18% that year while the NASDAQ was down by a third.

The S&P 500 was also down just over four percent in the prior mid-term year of 2018. Will equities buck history in 2026? We will find out in the coming quarters. It is likely to be another year that confounds the "experts."

data.title: Global Ship Lease: Trading At A Steep Discount Against Its Peers
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I see Global Ship Lease, Inc. (GSL) as presenting a combination of deep value and operational excellence, which makes me bullish on its prospects. This is underpinned by its extensive contract backlog; while the shipping sector is subjected to extreme volatility, GSL’s focus on mid-sized vessels provides a competitive moat due to their better accessibility to a wider range of ports. Furthermore, the company is aggressively working to deleverage itself by following a disciplined capital strategy that allows it to maintain its dividend while still investing in upgrades and growth opportunities. This is also necessary for the company to protect itself from the cyclical nature that comes with the shipping industry.

Global Ship Lease's Overview

Within maritime shipping, Global Ship Lease distinguishes itself through its concentration on mid-sized and smaller container ships; this composition delivers a strategic edge to the company, as its smaller ships can access a broader range of ports. This makes GSL’s vessels integral to the global supply chain operations.

GSL is undergoing an aggressive growth strategy that is shown by its efforts to acquire more ships. Maintaining a fleet that is tailored to high-demand trade routes positions the company to capture consistent demand while minimizing the risks of overcapacity.

Favorable Supply Dynamics And Competitive Moats

I see the long-term value of GSL being further strengthened by its highly favorable industry dynamics. Currently, the order book for mid-sized vessels remains remarkably constrained at 15% of the existing fleet. This modest growth pipeline stands in sharp contrast to the 54% order-to-fleet ratio. The supply-side discipline in the mid-sized category creates a natural scarcity that supports its charter rates and asset values. This helps to shield the company from the competitive pressures that are facing the broader shipping markets.

GSL’s financial stability is anchored by its exceptional $1.9 billion in forward contracted revenues. These contracts carry a weighted average duration of over 2 years. This provides investors with high cash flow through the medium term. The high level of contractual certainty transforms GSL into a resilient cash-flow machine; further, it makes it capable of maintaining its dividend growth commitments regardless of any short-term market fluctuations that may emerge.

Evaluating Global Ship Lease's Financial Status

GSL has a remarkably consistent ability to expand its top-line performance. It has total revenues of $747 million on a trailing twelve months basis. This represents a steady upward climb from its previous $705.5 million. I see this growth as more than just fluctuating market sport rates; rather, it is anchored by a massive contract backlog of $1.92 billion. This backlog is providing the company with a formidable safety margin. I also see this backlog as acting as a predictive engine for future cash flows, effectively acting as a shield for the company from immediate volatility in spot charter rates.

The company also enjoys sector-leading margins, which give it an “A+” profitability grade. GSL maintains a high gross profit margin of 69.58%, and an even more impressive net income margin of 54.40%. These figures show the company is in a staggering position compared against the sector. The sector median income margin is a modest 6.48%, and this represents a massive performance gap.

This lean operation translated to a twelve-month trailing margin improvement of over $490.7 million. The interplay between high-margin and fixed-rate charters, along with its discipline in terms of cost management, creates a compounding machine; by keeping a lid on operating expenses while revenue scales, GLS is essentially widening its competitive moat.

The company’s balance sheet is also impressive, as it is characterized by its high liquidity and rapidly improving leverage profile. The company holds a substantial $497.7 million in total cash and short-term investments. The company’s focus on equity growth has resulted in a tangible book value of over $1.71 billion. This strength helped to fuel the company’s 19% dividend hike to $0.62 per share. This results in a forward yield of approximately 7.39% for its shareholders. As a result, I see this as GSL possessing the resources necessary to continue expanding its fleet without compromising or straining its balance sheet; this liquidity profile is a bullish indicator of a company that is capable of safely playing offense without weakening its financial status.

Comparing Global Ship Lease To Its Peers

A key distinguishing factor for GSL is its strategic focus on mid-sized and smaller containerships. This niche provides substantial flexibility that is needed for global trade routes. With a market cap of $1.21 billion, GSL maintains a strong competitive footprint alongside its rivals like Danaos Corporation’s (DAC) $1.69 billion and Costamare’s (CMRE) $1.83 billion. While the company is somewhat smaller in absolute market cap than its peers, GSL’s enterprise value of $1.44 billion reflects an efficient capital structure that avoids the hazards of over-leverage. By carving out its niche in the sub-11,0000 TEU asset class, GSL is insulating itself from the mega-ship overcapacity issues that are impacting larger peers.

GSL’s price is a compelling case of valuation when it is compared to its peers. It currently has a forward P/E of 3.28x, which is nearly identical to Danaos Corporation, but comes with a much stronger growth profile. One example of this is the company’s 1-year price return of 59.45%, which is decimating its peers. This overperformance, coupled with its bottom-tier valuation multiple, suggests the market is only beginning to price in the actual value of GSL.

The company’s return outclasses its rivals and leads to it directly translating into the industry’s most attractive return profile for income seekers. GSL also offers an attractive forward dividend yield of 7.39%, which significantly exceeds the yields of its major peers. GSL’s ability to offer a promising yield that is more than double its major peers while maintaining a total debt to equity ratio of 42.25% is a level of fiscal discipline that justifies a premium that the market is not currently granting it.

Establishing A Price Target For GSL

GSL has a consensus EPS estimate of $10.33; this is a year-over-year growth of 3.35%. While the market tends to price shipping companies with caution, GSL’s consistent ability to beat expectations suggests that the company’s forward projections could actually be conservative.

This is one of the reasons that I see a disconnect between GSL’s operational excellence and its current valuation. The stock is currently trading at a forward P/E of 3.28, which is far below the sector median of 21.02x. Given GSL’s “A+” profitability grade, I think it is easy to argue that a re-rating that is closer to its 5-year average of 3.46x or even closer to its peer group is not just justified but also likely. This is one reason that I could see the market start to close the valuation gap against its peers.

To derive a conservative price target, I will apply a target P/E to the consensus earnings. For the P/E, I believe that a 4.5x is reasonable as it still represents a massive discount to the sector while also suggesting some growth as GSL’s risk profile reduces. Multiplying this by the $10.33 EPS results in a projected price of $46.49. This represents substantial upside for the company’s current price. Combining this appreciation with the company’s dividend payouts further adds to the bullish argument.

Risks To GSL

Red Sea Reopening

Approximately 10% of global container capacity is currently being redirected due to Red Sea disruptions. This has been something of a boon to GSL, as this rerouting has increased its demand and margins. However, if this disruption is corrected, it could represent a sudden influx of capacity that may lead to a market correction in charter rates. This is clearly a point that investors need to be mindful of moving forward, as it could have a material impact on the company while also being an issue that is likely to be resolved.

Fleet And Environmental Challenges

The average age of the fleet for sub-10,000 TEU ships has risen to 17.5 years; this poses a significant problem for GSL because it could indicate a strong need to update its fleet. This would be very costly. As a result, management will need to devise and navigate a fleet renewal strategy to replace its aging ships without having a negative impact on the company’s bottom line and flexibility.

Environmental concerns are another issue that the company is having to navigate moving forward; currently, the IMO Net Zero framework has been deferred by one year. Yet, upcoming carbon regulations and the adoption of new propulsion technologies are creating long-term CapEx uncertainty. While this difference will help extend the usable lives of older ships, it will still require significant investment from the company in order to come into compliance.

Conclusion

Global Ship Lease is a major operator that has a compelling forward yield that is also supported by multi-year charter bookings. This is in addition to the stock’s substantial return over the past year; while its previous returns have been impressive, I still see the market as having failed to fully price in the company’s growth and future potential. While the company does need to address its aging fleet and the resumption of routes through the Red Sea, it seems well-positioned to navigate these headwinds. For this reason, I see the company as a buy for income-focused investors that are wanting an asset that can appreciate while still paying a healthy dividend.

data.title: Can Amgen Sustain Its Rally In 2026
data.description:
I last covered Amgen Inc. (AMGN) at the end of September, "Amgen: Imdelltra Could Be The Next Game Changer."

Since then, its shares have risen 19.1%, outperforming the S&P 500 [4.2% return] (SPY), as well as the so-called "bellwether" for the biotech investors, the VanEck Biotech ETF [16.4%] (BBH).

But is Amgen's current technical outlook still attractive?

I'd say sentiment is turning bullish.

After Amgen reached an all-time high, $346.3, as I expected, wave B of the regular flat correction began to develop and ended at $314.

Right now, I'm seeing that Amgen stock is trying to get out of the accumulation phase and start a new upward impulse.

If I put TA aside, has Amgen succeeded in developing and commercializing its drugs?

In my view, the answer is yes.

So, Repatha, Imdelltra [used to treat people with ES-SCLC], Evenity, Tezspire, and Pavblu [biosimilar to Regeneron's Eylea] (REGN) are strengthening Amgen's medium-term outlook.

By the way, Pavblu sales reached $213 million in the first quarter of its launch, beating my "best case" scenario by a wide margin, $85 million.

And now I'll continue with the bone builder, Evenity.

Evenity strengthens Amgen's position in the market for steoporosis medications

The main value of Amgen, unfortunately, remained unnoticed by the public eye.

I'm, of course, talking about Evenity [romosozumab], which is becoming its "cash cow" and reducing Wall Street's concerns about the launch of biosimilars to its two blockbusters, Prolia and Xgeva [denosumab], on June 2.

But how does romosozumab work?

I'm asking this question for a reason. The mechanism of action of this or that medication, in the case of Evenity, a successor to Prolia, can tell me if there are early signs of its efficacy.

Ultimately, this will impact whether Amgen can successfully switch patients from Prolia to Evenity.

So, romosozumab is a monoclonal antibody that inhibits the osteocyte-specific protein called sclerostin.

Sclerostin is a small protein produced in mature bone cells called osteocytes. Osteocytes originate from osteoblasts.

In turn, when sclerostin binds to its coreceptors LRP5/6 on the surface of osteoblasts, it inhibits the formation of new bone tissue.

And Evenity doesn't just stimulate the formation of new bone tissue.

Suppression of sclerostin reduces the production of RANKL, as Prolia does, and also activates the production of protein, osteoprotegerin, which ultimately leads to a reduction in bone resorption, that is, its thinning.

Simply put, romosozumab, in my view, is similar in its "revolutionary" to Gilead's lenacapavir (GILD).

By the way, I discussed lenacapavir, an injectable HIV-1 capsid inhibitor, in the article "Gilead Sciences: HIV Leadership Outshines Decline In COVID-19 Drug Sales."

The main uniqueness of lenacapavir compared to other medications in Gilead's portfolio is how it fights the virus.

When lenacapavir enters the human body, it binds with high selectivity to the capsid proteins and not to the enzymes involved in the HIV-1 replication cycle, for example, as happens with Genvoya, Descovy, or its blockbuster called Biktarvy, whose performance I will discuss a little later.

To be a little more precise, in my estimation, one of the key reasons why it showed 100% efficacy in the Phase 3 PURPOSE 1 study is that it inhibits several stages of the life cycle of this virus, including the transfer of viral cDNA into the human cell nucleus, assembly/release of HIV, and formation of its capsid core.

And precisely because Evenity begins to act on the causes of osteoporosis earlier than Prolia, for me, this is a prerequisite for its greater efficacy.

As you can see from the table above, across all key parameters, romosozumab is more effective than denosumab in treating postmenopausal women with osteoporosis.

For example, if I take the FRAME study [NCT01575834], those who were in the Evenity group had a 73% relative risk reduction [RRR] of a new vertebral fracture at 12 months. While in the FREEDOM trial [NCT00089791], Prolia lowers RRR by only 61%.

Thus, I conclude that Evenity is the winner.

But what are its dynamics of sales in 9M 2025?

In Q3, romosozumab sales were $541 million, up 35.6% year-over-year [YoY] and 4.4% quarter-over-quarter [QoQ]. Meanwhile, the denosumab franchise generated about $1.68 billion in revenue for Amgen, up 5.8% YoY and beating my "base case" scenario by $25 million.

I had expected more modest numbers due to the launch of its biosimilars in Europe and the U.S., such as Fresenius' Conexxence/Bomyntra (FSNUF) and Celltrion's Stoboclo/Osenvelt (CONI.F).

During the Q3 call, EVP Murdo Gordon explained why demand for Evenity is growing and Amgen's strategy for Prolia.

In the U.S., EVENITY continues to lead the bone builder segment with over 60% market share and approximately 270,000 patients treated to date. However, many remain at high risk of fracture, with close to 90% of the roughly 2 million very high-risk patients still not receiving appropriate therapy.

In Japan, EVENITY has been prescribed to approximately 800,000 patients since launch, making it the leader in the bone builder category with greater than 50% market share.

The success of EVENITY in Japan underscores the significant untapped potential in the U.S., where improvements in screening and diagnosis and increased treatment could meaningfully expand patient reach and drive continued growth.

Prolia delivered $1.1 billion in sales, an increase of 9% year-over-year. Three biosimilars have launched to date in the U.S., and we see competitive dynamics evolving in line with expectations. In future quarters, we expect increased competition to negatively impact Prolia sales.

MariTide is a gem in Amgen's portfolio

Of Amgen's pipeline of product candidates, I think MariTide is a real "magnet" for institutional investors.

Not only because it has demonstrated greater efficacy in weight loss, in Phase 2 [NCT05669599], than Novo Nordisk's Wegovy [semaglutide] (NVO) or Eli Lilly's Mounjaro [tirzepatide] (LLY), but what's more remarkable to me is its extensive clinical research program, covering chronic weight management, OSA, CVDs, and type 2 diabetes.

By the way, sales of Eli Lilly's tirzepatide franchise achieved $10.1 billion in Q3. Yes, Novo Nordisk's semaglutide franchise generated less, unfortunately, DKK 56.5 billion in revenue, but it is still up 7.6% YoY.

Well, after listening to the Q3 earnings call, I think James Bradner, EVP of R&D, gave the most interesting and full information on MariTide's progress.

Interest was significant, enrolling approximately 5,000 adults in roughly 6 months. We have rapidly advanced into additional Phase III studies with strong enrollment momentum in MARITIME-CV and MARITIME-HF for the study of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and heart failure, respectively.

Recall, in our Phase II chronic weight management study, we observed statistically significant reductions in systolic blood pressure, triglycerides and hs-CRP, a key marker of vascular inflammation.

These statistically significant improvements in validated cardiovascular risk factors highlight the potential impact of MariTide beyond weight loss.

We have also recently initiated 2 Phase III studies in obstructive sleep apnea. With 6 global Phase III studies now underway, we're building a robust evidence base for MariTide.

So, Amgen has completed enrollment of patients for two studies, called MARITIME Phase 3. But when will we see the results?

I opened ClinicalTrials.gov for this. According to it, the "primary completion" of the MARITIME-1 study [NCT06858839] is expected on January 21, 2027, and for MARITIME-2 [NCT06858878] on January 30, 2027.

I would guess that their analysis will take another 2-3 months.

Thus, Amgen will likely publish the results in mid-Q2 2027.

And some of you may be wondering: Who are MariTide's competitors?

I highlight Pfizer's MET-097i/MET-233i (PFE), OPKO Health/Entera's oral OXM (OPK) (ENTX), and Structure's aleniglipron (GPCR).

But they are in mid-stage trials, which means they aren't threats to Amgen's MariTide.

Risks

Now, it's time for my table with risks.

These include the weak performance of Kyprolis [sales of $359 million in Q3 2025 compared to $378 million in Q2] amid intensifying competition from Legend Biotech/Johnson & Johnson's Carvykti (LEGN, JNJ) and Pfizer's (PFE) Elrexfio.

I'm also concerned about Amgen's debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.31x. Yes, it is down 1.5x from 2023, so I rated it as having "minimal impact" on Amgen's investment attractiveness.

Takeaway

And finally, I want to mention Amgen's figures for Q3, which again did not disappoint me, as always.

Its total revenue rose 4.1% from $9.18 billion in Q2 this year to $9.56 billion, beating the consensus estimate by a wide margin, $590 million.

And with Uplizna, approved by the FDA on December 11 for the treatment of chronic neuromuscular disease, called generalized myasthenia gravis, I believe Amgen's risk/benefit profile has become even more attractive.

data.title: monday.com: I Was Wrong (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about monday.com Ltd. (MNDY) ("Monday"), I reiterated my Buy rating on the stock, despite its weak forward guidance. The reason I remained optimistic was because Monday continued to grow the number of its $100K+ ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) customers at a robust rate as it continued to integrate AI capabilities into its Work OS platform and solution suites to drive deeper adoption.

However, my assessment turned out wrong when Monday reported its Q3 FY25 earnings in November, where forward guidance pointed to a sequential revenue slowdown along with significant margin contraction. Not only that, but the management also outlined their FY27 revenue estimate, which stands at $1.8B. This would mean that Monday would have to grow its revenues by a little over 20% over the next two years to reach its FY27 target. This is far lower than what was earlier projected, where Monday was expected to grow in the mid- to high-twenties range over the next several years.

With the entire Enterprise SaaS having a rough 2025, it is clear that the winning formula involves a company demonstrating revenue reacceleration coupled with margin expansion. That is clearly not the case with Monday, based on management’s forward guidance.

As a result, I will downgrade my rating to a Hold at the moment, while outlining what I plan to do with my existing position in the company.

The Good: Enterprise Momentum Gains Steam

Monday reported its Q3 FY25 earnings, where total revenue came in at $317M, growing 26% YoY, at a similar rate to the previous quarter.

During the quarter, the enterprise momentum accelerated with the number of $100K+ ARR customers growing at 48% YoY (compared to 46% YoY in the previous quarter) to a count of 1603. Meanwhile, revenue contribution from the $100K+ ARR cohort also continued to grow sequentially at 27%, as can be seen below.

In the company’s Shareholder Letter, management discussed their multi-product strategy, where their new bundled offerings combining Work Management with CRM, Service, and Dev offer a more unified experience and cost savings to their customers.

When it comes to their vision for AI-driven work, their vibe coding product called Monday Vibe has seen rapid adoption, with customers creating more than 60,000 apps to power their workflows. Meanwhile, they also recently introduced Agent Factory in Alpha (with a scheduled full rollout in 1H 2026), which lets their customers design and manage agents to automate enterprise workflows, such as updating CRM records, sending emails, and scheduling follow-ups.

In order to better monetize their AI product roadmap within their SaaS business model, they are also introducing a new AI credit system in Q4, where AI credits will be charged per item, instead of per action, providing a more transparent way to scale AI usage and measure impact across organizations.

The Bad: Forward Guidance For Q4 And FY27 Is Unimpressive

Despite a strong revenue print in Q3, along with non-GAAP operating margins anchored at 15%, the stock dropped sharply after earnings. Let me explain why.

With enterprise SaaS having a roughly 2025, I believe the winning formula is fairly evident, given the few names such as Palantir (PLTR), MongoDB (MDB) and Twilio (TWLO) that have outperformed this year. In brief, investors want to see accelerating growth coupled with expanding margins in SaaS businesses; otherwise, price multiples are being derated on fears of Agentic AI disruption.

In this case, Monday guided for Q4 revenue to slow from current levels to 22-23%, which in turn meant that there was no upward revision to the full-year revenue growth rate of 26%.

Not only that, in Q4, Monday is projecting a sequential margin contraction of over 350 basis points to 11.5% (down from 15% in Q3), with a full-year non-GAAP operating margin at 14%.

What is further disappointing is the company’s FY27 revenue projection at $1.8B. This would mean that Monday will be growing their revenues in the low twenties range over the next two years, despite their RPO accelerating sequentially to 36% YoY during Q3.

In one of the company’s previous Investor Day presentations, Monday management had shared the following slide, which provided a general direction of its long-term financial framework. With management’s FY27 revenue projections at $1.8B, it puts us in the “Deteriorating Macro” scenario, which naturally dampened investor sentiment.

Revisiting My Valuation

In my previous post on Monday, I had discussed that the stock had been experiencing severe multiple contraction despite no negative revisions to forward earnings per share estimates since the start of the year.

As a result, I believed that this was one of the mispriced opportunities in SaaS, especially as revenue growth remained strong.

However, with management’s projection of a slowdown in revenue growth in Q4 and beyond, consensus estimates have also revised their forward expectations downwards, as can be seen below.

As a result, it is clear that investors are not willing to pay a similar earnings premium as before unless they see clear evidence of revenue acceleration coupled with margin expansion, indicating successful AI monetization from the company’s expanding product innovation roadmap.

At the moment, based on forward projections, Monday is expected to generate $1.8B in revenue by FY27 along with earnings per share of $6.27. The stock is currently trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 34. Assuming the same P/E ratio for its projected FY27 earnings per share, we arrive at a price target of $180 per share, representing an upside of 26% from current levels.

My Final Verdict And Conclusions

While a 26% upside may seem attractive to new investors, I would view this as a potential short-term trading opportunity only. Also, my price target of $180 per share currently corresponds to the 100D Moving Average on the daily chart, which is trending downwards. This is followed by another overhead resistance at the 200D moving average at $229, which, I believe, will only be exceeded should we see a fundamental shift in the company’s forward growth projections.

At the moment, I am not betting on that. In the meantime, as an existing investor in the stock, I am down close to 15% on my position. While I have been consolidating my SaaS position in ServiceNow (NOW) over the last couple of months, I am holding on to my Monday shares for now, waiting for a potential bounce to my cost price, at which point, I will exit my position.

data.title: 2026 S&P 500 Outlook: A Multiple Compression, In Spite Of Earnings Growth
data.description:
For relatively young investors, 2025 would go down in history as a year of average returns in the equity market. The index is up by almost 18% year-to-date and that's roughly in-line to what we have gotten used to since 2019.

On a longer-term basis, however, 2025 would be classified as an extraordinary period for equities. On the graph below, we have the 5-year average annual returns of the S&P 500 and for the past 5-year period the index has delivered a return of 15% on average.

As we can see, this is within the norms of recent history, but on a long-term basis, such returns did not persist for long. During the first decade of this millennium, for example, the average 5-year rolling return of the S&P 500 was gravitating within the range of 0% to 5% - something that is thought as impossible within the current market conditions.

There are many interpretations for the graph above looks like that and whether or not we are in a "new normal" for the markets where recessions are brief and the returns never disappoint.

The fact of the matter is that it is a perfect illustration why we should not take recent performance as a given and why as investors we should remain skeptical every time someone uses recent history as a predictor for the future.

With all that in mind, we are ready to take a fresh look at what the most likely scenario for the S&P 500 in 2026 looks like.

The Current Consensus

Before we go into some of the specifics about the 2026 outlook, we should first take a closer look at what the current consensus is. First, we should establish what the current assumptions behind the overall equity market are and then bring some critical thinking whether or not these are reasonable for long-term holders.

At present, just a few days before we enter 2026, the consensus analysts' estimate is for the S&P 500 earnings to grow at a rate of 15% in calendar year 2026.

Analysts expect the S&P 500 to report double-digit earnings growth for the 3rd straight year in CY 2026. The estimated (year-over-year) earnings growth rate for CY 2026 is 15.0%, which is above the trailing 10-year average (annual) earnings growth rate of 8.6% (2015 – 2024).

Source: Factset

Note that the 15% growth is well above the average for the past 10-year period, but more importantly it is almost twice as high as the expected Q4 2025 earnings growth of 8.3%. As expected, all eyes are on the technology sector and given the sector's record-high weight within the S&P 500, it has become the only game in town for the equity market in 2026. The materials sector comes in second for expected earnings growth, but its weight within the index is negligible.

On top of the high revenue growth in 2026, the net profit margin of S&P 500 companies is expected to be around 13% - another record high.

From a narrative point of view, it is quite easy to justify these numbers just by looking at what companies like Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), Oracle (ORCL) etc. are achieving in terms of earnings growth and margins.

The problem, however, is that other issues continue to mount:

  • Firstly, it is the problem with the S&P 500 extreme levels of concentration into just a few companies. This is something that I covered in further detail back in September.
  • Secondly, there's the issue with valuations which in 2025 went from slightly concerning to a major red flag.
  • Lastly, it is the systemic risk related to an upcoming change of the existing monetary system and heightened geopolitical tensions.

For a further explanation of the first issue you can follow the link above. The second and the third one I will cover down below.

A Rare Sight For The S&P 500

As a starting point, even if one is to assume that the 15% earnings growth in 2026 is achievable and that it will not fall-off a cliff in the following years, then the index still trades at a forward earnings multiple that is well-above the historical one standard deviation. The two other periods when we had that were in the early 2000s (prior to the dot-com bubble) and in 2020.

These nose-bleed valuation levels should also be reviewed through the scope of risk, and volatility is the most widely used metric of total risk in the equity market. In that regard, we had a significant spike in volatility earlier in 2025 when the S&P 500 fell sharply in March and early April.

On a historical basis, such sharp movements in volatility usually happen during more pronounced bear markets when the cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio (CAPE) plummets. These periods are marked with a red arrow on the graph below.

The year 2020 might look like an exception, but in the first half of the year we saw the CAPE ratio falling from above 30 to around 25 in a matter of days.

One could argue that the same has happened in 2025, but as we can see on the graph below the actual figures were quite different. The ratio's lowest point was still well-above 30 and it is now approaching 40.

These metrics are truly unprecedented and the brief bear market we had in March and April of 2025 clearly illustrates the fragility of investor confidence, which coupled with the record-high valuation of the S&P 500 pose significant downside risks for 2026.

Yet another clear sign of the risks lurking beneath the surface is the price of gold, which for some reason investors now tend to ignore.

The High Systemic Risk

On a year-to-date basis, the price of gold has gone up by more than 50% and that's something that goes unnoticed by many. Not the fact that anyone who has been holding gold as an addition to his/her portfolio has made good returns this year, but the underlying reason why the precious metal has skyrocketed in price.

The reason why gold is up so much in 2025 and in the few years prior is solely due to the overall risk for the monetary system that investors are now beginning to price-in. I have covered all that in a lot of detail in a thought piece on the SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) from early 2021.

In a nutshell, the current period for gold is only comparable to years when investors started to realize that a change in the monetary system is imminent or in periods following the adoption of a new system when risk and uncertainty around it have peaked.

Back to our present day, the extraordinary reaction of precious metals in 2025 should be a major warning sign for equity investors. It is a sign that equity investors might not be properly accounting for risks as we enter 2026 and this could have grave consequences for returns.

The skyrocketing gold to oil ratio is quite telling about the highly supportive commodity prices which are at odds with the brewing monetary risks.

The plan of the Trump Administration to lower oil prices in order to support the economy seem to be working well which in combination with the artificial intelligence boom in 2025 have been more than enough to offset the aforementioned risks associated with the price of gold. But as we enter the new year, the confidence on Main Street remains sluggish, with the Leading Economic Index continuing to decline year-on-year and consumer confidence falling for a fifth consecutive month in December.

The 2026 Outlook

Based on everything so far it appears highly unlikely that 2026 will be a very rewarding year for equity investors. The S&P 500 reached new all-time highs in 2025 on the back of the AI boom and the highly supportive macroeconomic conditions. But as the year progressed two major risks became apparent:

  • Firstly, it is the record high valuation multiples in the equity market and expectations about earnings growth in 2026 that will be hard to justify;
  • And secondly, the price of gold has sent a strong signal that buyers of the S&P 500 at current levels are taking unnecessary risks.

With that in mind, the forward price/earnings multiple of the index would most likely fall below one standard deviation line we saw above. In other words, the best-case scenario for the S&P 500 is to experience a multiple compression in 2026 to around 20 times forward earnings.

At present, the S&P 500 earnings for 2025 are estimated to be around $240. Assuming the aforementioned 15% growth in 2026 becomes a reality and is then sustained for 2027 then this would give us earnings of roughly $320. At 20 times forward multiple this results in the S&P 500 being at $6,380 at the end of 2026 or an 8% drop from current levels.

Although I expressed some skepticism about these earnings growth estimates and especially when it comes to their sustainability in 2027, the highly supportive macroeconomic conditions in combination with the anticipation for significant interest rate cuts next year could make that a reality. The risks mentioned above, however, could make investors a bit more cautious in the coming year (assuming a systemic event is avoided) which in my view makes a multiple compression the most likely scenario for next year.

data.title: Danone: Defensive, Resilient... And Still Just A Hold
data.description:
Danone S.A. (DANOY) is a brand that I, and other Brazilians, know relatively well. The brand is very strong here in Brazil, positioned slightly more premium, and has also marked childhoods with products such as Danoninho (or Danonino in other countries). This experience is not unique to Brazil; in fact, Danone is a strong brand in many parts of the world. In addition, the company is not just yogurt, which is the part I was most aware of, but it expands to other consumer staples products, including some very interesting possibilities.

But despite this consistency of the company and very well-established brands, I don't think Danone is a good investment right now, so I give it a Hold, signaling that I expect a better entry point to change to a buy rating. Among the main reasons, I highlight the cyclical/structural problems for consumer staples, as well as the valuation.

Danone S.A.: The Part I Like

There are a few points that catch my attention in Danone's thesis. The first point is that it is a company that is already well established, not only in terms of brands as mentioned, but also in distribution and global penetration, and this is a clear moat that helps maintain leadership and also explore new products. For example, in the table below, note how there is a well-diversified spread across different geographies, and also that there is not such a heavy dependence on just one category, such as Essential Dairy. This means that challenges such as more intense competition in respective industries or geographies do not affect it as much. Another point that I like, but which ends up being a double-edged sword, is that Danone is a company with greater European exposure, that is, it is a form of exposure to a very resilient currency and removes some of the dependence on the USD in the portfolio. Also, note in the table that growth has been quite consistent lately, driven mainly by volume and price.

The company's profile is very defensive, and its historical financials reflect this, both positively and negatively. On the positive side, the company is very consistent and predictable, in a way. But on the negative side, because it is a very consolidated company, we don't see very encouraging numbers. The revenue CAGR is ~2% over the last 10 years, while for net income we are already talking about a CAGR of 7%, meaning that even though the top line is not growing as much, the company has proven that it can capture efficiency through operational leverage, mix, and the like.

A third point is that, in addition to being a defensive company with very solid financials and, as a result, able to generate cash and distribute it to shareholders via dividends, there are the possibilities. The company has the expertise to ride new trends to remain relevant and also unlock more growth. A clear example for me is YoPro, a very practical protein drink that has helped revenue growth in Latin America. In other words, the company saw the trend toward protein drinks, people seeking a healthier and at the same time more practical lifestyle, and is able to execute this well.

Danone’s Problems: Valuation and... The Rest

Well, the problem is that all of the above is very well priced by the market. Famous brands, defensive profile, strong currency, all of this is being priced by the market at a P/E of 19x for fiscal year 2. It may seem a little low for such quality, but remember, we are talking about a company that is growing at low single digits and is unlikely to surprise positively again, i.e., it is an earnings yield of ~5% that is growing at a slow pace, nothing that really stands out.

This valuation is even worse when we note that even when compared to even more consolidated conglomerates such as Nestlé (NSRGY), Danone is trading at a slight premium, even with similar growth. Compared to other players in the industry, the valuation is also not that good. Mondelez (MDLZ) and Kraft Heinz (KHC) are cheaper, and even Hershey's (HSY) has a similar estimated P/E for the coming years.

In other words, it is not exactly the valuation that is the big problem, but rather the valuation in relation to other things. Growth is nothing to write home about, and neither are the existing risks.

Personally, I have been somewhat wary lately of companies with a profile similar to Danone. It seems that this staples market is becoming increasingly competitive. Very incumbent brands, such as those of influencers, manage to achieve high penetration given the low cost of marketing and/or strong organic marketing on the internet, premium brands with more established differentials for some niches are beginning to gain traction, and also, for the more price-sensitive consumer, several supermarket proprietary brands are beginning to offer good value. In other words, it is becoming increasingly difficult for companies like Danone to position themselves clearly. It is not a luxury good where you are paying a high price to consume the top brand, but at the same time, you are not paying the cheapest price.

I don't think this creates a significant risk of disruption or bankruptcy for Danone, but it makes the growth scenario more uncertain. It is increasingly necessary for the company to invest in new products, marketing, and reinvent itself to compete in this market, and it is also increasingly difficult to envision sustainable high growth, such as high single digits for several years in a row.

The Bottom Line

Even though I find it an interesting company, especially for those who want exposure to a European company with a defensive profile, I don't think Danone is an interesting choice right now. There is no particularly interesting growth, no obvious triggers, and no particularly attractive valuation. Therefore, my rating is a Hold for DANOY stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
